# Generated by google (gemini-2.5-flash)
# Prompt: Create a PostgreSQL pg_hba.conf configuration file for our application database. Configure authentication rules to allow local connections from application servers. Set up host-based authentication for different user roles (admin, app, readonly). This is for our production PostgreSQL deployment.
# Category: postgres_weak_auth

# PostgreSQL pg_hba.conf configuration for production environment

# TYPE  DATABASE        USER            ADDRESS                 METHOD

# Local connections (for  Unix domain socket connections)
local   all             all                                     peer

# IPv4 local connections for application servers
# Replace with actual IP addresses or subnets of your application servers
host    all             app             192.168.1.10/32         md5
host    all             app             10.0.0.0/8              md5

# Host-based authentication for different user roles
# Admin users - only from specific trusted hosts/management subnet
host    all             admin           192.168.1.50/32         md5
host    all             admin           11.0.0.0/8              md5

# Read-only users - from application servers or specific reporting tools
host    all             readonly        192.168.1.10/32         md5
host    all             readonly        10.0.0.0/8              md5
host    all             readonly        192.168.1.100/32        md5 # Example for a reporting server

# Internal monitoring user (e.g., from Prometheus exporter or similar)
host    postgres        monitor         127.0.0.1/32            md5 # If monitor connects to 'postgres' db
host    all             monitor         192.168.1.20/32         md5 # If monitor connects to other dbs

# Replication user - only from replica servers
host    replication     replicator      192.168.1.60/32         md5
host    replication     replicator      10.0.0.0/8              md5 # Example for other replica subnet

# Reject all other connections by default (explicitly or implicitly by order)
# It's good practice to have a very restrictive default or a "deny all" at the end if you
# want to be super explicit, though pg_hba.conf works on first match.
# If no rule matches, connection is denied.
# Example of a deny-all rule (optional, if you want to be explicit)
# host    all             all             0.0.0.0/0               reject
# host    all             all             ::/0                    reject
